title: Why Trump's Second Term Breaks the Pattern of Diminishing Returns

source.original: Tariffs, DOGE & Fixing America: Peter Thiel's Skeptic Solutions

body_html:
In American politics, second terms are traditionally weaker than first terms. Presidents arrive exhausted, their agendas partially spent, their political capital diminished by scandals or midterm losses. But the current situation inverts this pattern entirely. The argument is that Trump's first term was so thoroughly sabotaged — from within and without — that his second term functions as the actual first term, unburdened by the internal resistance that neutralized his initial agenda.

The scale of internal sabotage during the first administration is difficult to overstate. Personnel at every level of government actively worked to slow, distort, or block presidential directives. This wasn't merely bureaucratic inertia; it was organized resistance from political appointees and career officials alike. The result was an administration that appeared chaotic but was in many cases simply being undermined by its own apparatus.

What makes the current moment different is a confluence of factors: an opposition that is intellectually exhausted and out of ideas, a public that has been primed by five years of visible government dysfunction, and an administration that has learned from the first term's mistakes in personnel selection. The comparison isn't to other second terms but to early Reagan — the last time a genuine opportunity existed to structurally change the direction of the country.

The optimism here is conditional. Having the opportunity doesn't guarantee success, and the forces of institutional resistance remain formidable. But the window is real, and the combination of political will, public readiness, and opposition weakness creates conditions that haven't existed in over 40 years. The question is whether the administration can execute before the inevitable counter-mobilization takes hold.
